coredata.dc:title: Rapid conversion from common precursors to carbon dots in large scale: Spectral controls, optical sensing, cellular imaging and LEDs application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Qiao, Gongxi
Chen, Gui
Wen, Qin
Liu, Wanqiang
Gao, Jinwei
Yu, Zhiqiang
Wang, Qianming
coredata.dc:description: The commercial production of carbon dots will be concerned with the simplicity and energy consumption. Herein, maleic acid and m-phenylenediamine form elegantly simple sources for carbon dots. The two precursors are dissolved in formamid (abbreviated as FA) or N,N-dimethylformamide (abbreviated as DMF) and the dehydration-condensation processes have been performed at 30 min or 120 min under room temperature. No external energy/irradiations, reactants or high temperature will be required and the afforded carbon dots (abbreviated as CDs) are collected by extraction, centrifugation, dialysis and column chromatography. It has been found for the first time the choice of organic solvents has been correlated with emission color. The blue-emitting CDs (abbreviated as B-CDs) and green-emitting CDs (abbreviated as G-CDs) are yielded in FA and DMF respectively. Facts support that the increase of –CONH- units causes red-shift in emissions. The optical sensing of tetracycline has been explored and the detection limit of blue-emitting CDs is as low as 25 nM. Live cells exposed to B-CDs and G-CDs (0.5 mg/ml) show no apparent changes via both Cell Counting Kit-8 and Annexin V/7-AAD analysis. The blue and green fluorescent signals can be easily tracked in cells. It has been demonstrated that the two carbon dots can be fabricated as multiple-color light-emitting diodes (abbreviated as LEDs).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Room-temperature synthesis
Luminescence
Tetracycline
In vitro

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the Blue-emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) and Green-emitting carbon dots (G-CDs)
B-CDs fluorescence determination of tetracycline in aqueous solution
B-CDs fluorescence determination of tetracycline in human urine
Cell culture and cytotoxicity of the B-CDs and G-CDs
Cell apoptosis analysis of the B-CDs and G-CDs
Cell imaging of the B-CDs and G-CDs
Fabrication of blue light-emitting diodes (abbreviated as Blue-LEDs) and green light- emitting diodes (abbreviated as Green-LEDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the synthesis of the B-CDs and G-CDs.

A) TEM images of the B-CDs B) B-CDs have HRTEM pattern with lattice fringes. C) SAED pattern of B-CDs. D) Dynamic light scattering result of the B-CDs. E) TEM images of the G-CDs F) G-CDs have HRTEM pattern with lattice fringes. G) SAED pattern of G-CDs. H) Dynamic light scattering result of the G-CDs.
TEM images of B-CDs samples (A–F) and G-CDs sample (G–M) at different time duration.
A) XRD pattern of the B-CDs and G-CDs. B) EDS pattern of the BCDs and G-CDs.

A) UV–vis absorption spectra and Fluorescence spectra of the B-CDs. B) Fluorescence emission spectra of the B-CDs taken under different excitation wavelengths. C) UV–vis absorption spectra and Fluorescence spectra of the G-CDs. D) Fluorescence emission spectra of the G-CDs taken under different excitation wavelengths.
Illustration for the optical energy band gap transitions of the two carbon dots and plot of (α*hv)1/2 versus hv of the two carbon dots.

A) Fluorescence spectra of the B-CDs with addition of TC solution (0–23 μM). B) Fluorescent intensity ratio (F0-F)/F0) of B-CDs in the presence of tetracycline at different concentrations and linear fitting equations. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three replicate measurements.
HeLa cells labeled with annexin PE-Cy5/APC and analyzed by flow cytometry. A) and C) were control, B) were co-culture with the B-CDs (0.5 mg/mL), D) were co-culture with the G-CDs (0.5 mg/ml).
Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells co-cultured with the B-CDs and G-CDs after 6 h. (All channel excitation wavelength of 405 nm).
A) Emissions spectra of Blue-LEDs. B) Emissions spectra of Green-LEDs. C) Character parameters of the two LEDs. D) CIE color coordinates of two LEDs.

head.ce:title: Rapid conversion from common precursors to carbon dots in large scale: Spectral controls, optical sensing, cellular imaging and LEDs application

Hunan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center For Transparent Conductive Materials, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center For Transparent Conductive Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center For Transparent Conductive Materials, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The two authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the successful synthesis of B-CDs and G-CDs at room temperature via maleic acid and m-phenylenediamine as two precursors were reported. Unlike the previous literatures, the replacement of formamid with DMF could control the band shifts from blue to green. The nature of emission color evolvement has been substantiated by surface states. Their chemical composition, structure and photoluminescence were investigated and the relevant –CONH– components would be responsible for the shifts of emission wavelengths. Detailed fluorometric titration experiments revealed that tetracycline could cause emission quenching and the detection limit was as low as 25 nM. The two CDs were found to be low toxicity and exhibited potentials in cellular-imaging. In addition, the fabrication of LEDs devices was realized.

The commercial production of carbon dots will be concerned with the simplicity and energy consumption. Herein, maleic acid and m-phenylenediamine form elegantly simple sources for carbon dots. The two precursors are dissolved in formamid (abbreviated as FA) or N,N-dimethylformamide (abbreviated as DMF) and the dehydration-condensation processes have been performed at 30 min or 120 min under room temperature. No external energy/irradiations, reactants or high temperature will be required and the afforded carbon dots (abbreviated as CDs) are collected by extraction, centrifugation, dialysis and column chromatography. It has been found for the first time the choice of organic solvents has been correlated with emission color. The blue-emitting CDs (abbreviated as B-CDs) and green-emitting CDs (abbreviated as G-CDs) are yielded in FA and DMF respectively. Facts support that the increase of –CONH- units causes red-shift in emissions. The optical sensing of tetracycline has been explored and the detection limit of blue-emitting CDs is as low as 25 nM. Live cells exposed to B-CDs and G-CDs (0.5 mg/ml) show no apparent changes via both Cell Counting Kit-8 and Annexin V/7-AAD analysis. The blue and green fluorescent signals can be easily tracked in cells. It has been demonstrated that the two carbon dots can be fabricated as multiple-color light-emitting diodes (abbreviated as LEDs).

ce:section.ce:para: Sustainability has been considered as the favorable factor which will maintain the stability of human ecology, environment and daily life [1–5]. Accordingly, the development of functional materials and novel preparation techniques with cost-effective or energy saving feature has aroused considerable interests in scientific fields [6–10]. During the design of optical-active materials, extensive studies have been focused on the synthesis of heavy-metal free and less-polluted nanoplatforms [11–14]. In recent years, carbon nanomaterials can be employed for the application in diagnosis, sensing and optoelectronic devices due to their excellent photo-stability, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and variable luminescent signals [15–22].

As for the preparation process, the two different ways such as top-down and bottom-up approaches have been well described in literatures [23,24]. Compared with the decomposition of carbon allotropes, the bottom-up routes are generally free from surface passivation and the incorporation of heteroatoms into carbon nanostructures will be relatively easy. Although such strategy can produce carbon nanodots in large scale based on low-molecular-weight molecules or polymers, there are several obstacles to be circumvented before further exploration. At first, the current approaches are frequently relying on high temperature pyrolysis, external energy irradiations (microwave or ultarsonic) or hydrothermal treatment. For example, the Teflon-lined autoclave requires over night reaction at more than 100 °C and continuous electric power should be provided. Hence, the real efficient synthesis way for achieving carbon dots with intensive luminescence can arouse considerable interests [25–30]. Secondly, it is hard to clarify the relationship between the photoluminescence feature and the organic structure of carbon dots. The controlled preparation of high-quality nanomaterials based on rational design will be attractive. Especially the facile assembly way that can achieve carbon dots with effective fluorescent signals will be appealing to both theoretical study and practical uses.

It has been well documented that most of carbon dots give rise to blue luminescence upon the excitation at ultra-violet region and such emitting process is detrimental to living organisms or biological observations. In particular, cells or tissues with their carbohydrate structures usually emit in blue range and the signals will be overlapped with carbon dots. Therefore, there have been made great efforts to vary the emission light from blue to green [31–34]. In this contribution, a rapid, simple and effective method for assembling carbon dots has been developed based on chemical oxidative polymerization processes. No excessive energy supply or sophisticated apparatus is included. During the synthesis process, m-phenylenediamine and maleic acid were dissolved in formamid (FA) with stirring for only 30 min at room temperature (25 °C) and the blue-emitting carbon dots were generated (quantum yield = 42%). Unexpectedly, the replacement of formamid (FA) with N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) could induce emission change from blue band at 460 nm to green emission at 500 nm (quantum yield = 35%). In addition, the utilization of two molecular precursors was a useful strategy to produce carbon dots with yields (25% for blue dots and 22% for green dots). It would be relatively easy to get the target material at gram scale. In terms of the energy dispersive spectrometer analysis, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (abbreviated as FT-IR) and optical energy band gap calculation, the origins of photoluminescence from two carbon dots have been fully explored. The results revealed that the nitrogen/oxygen content and surface oxidation state caused the red shift of the emission wavelength. So far as the extension to application is concerned, fluorescence signal is still the preferential feature of carbon dots for the monitoring of molecules. The absorption band of tetracycline was overlapped with the excitation spectra of carbon dots. Consequently, the emission was quenched due to the possible inner filter effect. Moreover, the potential as cellular imaging agents has also been evaluated. In contrast to common organic fluorophores, these carbon dots were more photostable and demonstrated unique emissions in live cells. More importantly, the excitation-dependence property was also verified during in vitro studies and the fluorescence could be controlled from blue to green via the change of channels of microscope (from blue channel to green channel). Except the above promising prospects of molecule recognition and cellular staining, their performance in optoelectronic devices was also explored. The CIE coordinates of two LEDs (blue and green) were recorded.

Maleic acid (99%), m-phenylenediamine (99%), formamid (FA, 99%), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 98%) were provided by Macklin Reagent Compant (Shanghai, China). Tetracyclinehydrate (abbreviated as TC, 96%), penicillin (99%), ampicillin (96%), streptomycin (99%), lincomycin hydrochloride (95%), cefalexin hydrate (98%), hemoglobin (99%), sparfloxacin (98%), marbofloxacin (98%), glucose (98%) and l-alanine, (Ala, 98%), l-aspartic acid (Asp, 98%), L(+)-arginine (Arg, 99%), l-asparagine(Asn, 99%), l-cysteine (Cys, 99%), l-glutamine (Gln, 99%), Glycine (Gly, 99%), l-histidine (His, 99%), homocysteine (Hcy, 95%) , l-leucine (Leu, 99%), l-lysine (Lys, 98.5%), dl-serine (Ser, 98%) and l-tryptophan (Trp, 99%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco’s modified medium (abbreviated as DMEM) and RPMI-1640 (Roswell Park Memorial Institute-1640) were purchased from Cytiva company (United States). Fetal bovine serum (abbreviated as FBS) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific company (United States). Cell Counting Kit-8 (abbreviated as CCKit-8) was purchased from Dojindo Molecular Technologies, Inc (Japan). Annexin V-PE/7-AAD Apoptosis Detection Kit (abbreviated as Annexin V/7-AAD Kit) was provided by Becton, Dickinson and Company (United States). All the other reagents including ultrapure water were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China) and used without further purification.

Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of carbon dots were recorded by a UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a F-4600 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime of carbon dots was measured using a FLS 920-fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Britain). The morphology and size of carbon dots were observed using a JEM1400 High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JEOL, Japan). Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) was obtained by a Quantax 400 X-ray energy spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected by a Bruker D8/Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). FT-IR spectra were measured within the 4000–400 cm−1 region on an infrared spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, United States), by using the KBr pellet technique. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) results were recorded by a Zetasizer NanoZS instrument (ZEN 3600, Malvern, Britain). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded via a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, Britain). Molecular weight was evaluated by gel permeation chromatography (GPC) on a Water 1525 instrument (Milford, United States). All error bars represent standard deviations from three repeated experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the Blue-emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) and Green-emitting carbon dots (G-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Maleic acid (464.0 mg, 4 mmol) and m-phenylenediamine (324.0 mg, 3 mmol) were dispersed in 10 ml formamid in a 15 ml flask. After stirring for 30 min, the resulting solution was mixed with 40 ml of ethyl acetate in a separating funnel. After separation, the upper solution was extracted. The residual unreacted material was removed by using a cellulose ester dialysis membrane (Molecular weight cut off (MWCO): 500–1000) during a 24 h dialysis procedure. The collected solution was then freeze dried, the powder was dissolved in methanol and further purified by column chromatography (mobile phase, CH3OH:CH2Cl2 = 10:1 and solid phase, 300–400 mesh silica gel) to obtain the pure B-CDs solution. Finally, the B-CDs powder is obtained by freezing drying. The achieved product was stored in the icebox (−5°).

The preparation of the G-CDs was similar to B-CDs and formamid was replaced by N,N-dimethylformamide. After stirring for 2 h, the resulting solution was treated by using a cellulose ester dialysis membrane (MWCO: 500) for more than 24 h dialysis to obliterated unreacted material. After lyophilization, the precipitate was dissolved in methanol and further purified by column chromatography to obtain the pure G-CDs solution. Finally, the G-CDs powder was obtained by freezing drying and resultant product was stored in the icebox (−5°).

ce:section.ce:section-title: B-CDs fluorescence determination of tetracycline in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: During the fluorescence detection of tetracycline, The B-CDs powder (50 μg/ml) was dispersed in an aqueous solution. Then, different concentrations of tetracycline aqueous solution were added to the B-CDs solution to observe changes in fluorescence intensity with same excitation wavelength. For comparison, the sensitivity and selectivity experiments were also performed with other antibiotic and glucose under the same condition. Moreover, to assess the effects of other common interfering substances on tetracycline detection, various metal ions, anions, and amino acids were introduced with the identical concentration of 400 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: B-CDs fluorescence determination of tetracycline in human urine
ce:section.ce:para: Human urine samples (South China Normal University Hospital, China) were collected for recovery experiments. After the obtained sample was centrifuged, the supernatant was taken and packaged in a test tube. Finally, different concentrations of tetracycline were injected into each tube for fluorescence detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and cytotoxicity of the B-CDs and G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Hela cells (cervical cancer cells) were cultured (37 °C, 5% CO2) in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and penicillin (1000 units/ml)-streptomycin (10,000 μg/mL) solution. The effects of the two carbon dots on cell viability were measured using a more stable and rapid Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8). Hela cells (5000 cell/well) in 96-well plates were placed culture for 24 h. After the medium was removed, the cells were incubated with fresh medium containing blue-emitting carbon dots (or green-emitting carbon dots) at a concentration of xxx for another 24 h. Thereafter, 10 μL of CCK-8 solution was added to each well. Finally, after 4 h of culture, the absorbance at 490 nm was collected. Cell viability was calculated according to the following formula.
Cell
v i a b i l i t y =
O
D
CDs
- O
D
PBS
O
D
control
- O
D
PBS

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell apoptosis analysis of the B-CDs and G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of blue-emitting carbon dots (or green-emitting carbon dots) uptake on apoptosis was detected by flow cytometry. After the medium was removed, the cells were incubated with fresh medium of blue-emitting carbon dots (or green-emitting carbon dots) (500 μg/ml) for another 24 h. The sample was washed twice with binding buffer. The following treatment was centrifugal separation and another 100 μL of binding buffer was added. Finally, 3 μL Annexin V/7-AAD were added to each sample. Samples were cultured for 15 min and analyzed by flow cytometry on the BD FACS Calibur flow cytometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging of the B-CDs and G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Hela cells in chamber Lab-Tek coverslips (4-hole, Thermo Scientific, United States) were cultured. When Hela cell density was more than 70% of the culture plate, blue-emitting carbon dots (or green-emitting carbon dots) (0.5 mg/mL) were added in the culture medium. After incubation for 6 h, the cells were washed with a phosphate buffered saline (abbreviated as PBS) buffer solution for three times to remove excess amounts of carbon dots. Finally, the images were taken by fixing the sample on the microscope slide for observation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of blue light-emitting diodes (abbreviated as Blue-LEDs) and green light- emitting diodes (abbreviated as Green-LEDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet light-emitting-diodes (UV-LED) chip with a peak of 365 nm was selected, and the B-CDs were dispersed in an ethanol solution. Then, 40 ml of the ethanol solution was dropped onto the inner wall of the optical lens, and dried at 80 °C. Finally, the dried optical lens was laid on the UV-LEDs chip to obtain Blue-LEDs. Green-LEDs were prepared in a similar experimental procedure as described above.

Conversion from a few organic precursors into carbon dots has been extensively investigated by hydrothermal method or external irradiations [35,36]. Although the well-defined nanostructures could be afforded, it will still be valuable to rapidly achieve functional carbon dots without any assistance. In this work, blue-emissive carbon dots (B-CDs) and green-emissive carbon dots (G-CDs) could be effectively prepared by mixing m-phenylenediamine and maleic acid at room temperature. Formamid (FA) and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were employed as the solvents respectively. The cross-linking process included the dehydration and condensation reaction due to the amidation reaction between the abundant functional groups of the raw materials (Fig. 1 ).

The formation of B-CDs and G-CDs was explored by microstructure analysis as provided in Fig. 2 . The two nanomaterials possessed good dispersibility and the particles were observed to be spherical shapes. It has been found that the particle size distribution of B-CDs was around 5 nm, which was larger than the diameter of G-CDs (Fig. 2A, D). Fig. 2B, E gave high-resolution images of B-CDs and G-CDs. The two figures exhibited high crystallinity, with distinct lattice fringes. A lattice spacing of 2.1 Å was consistent with the d spacing of graphite (1,0,0) planes. Moreover, diffraction rings were also supported by selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns (Fig. 2C, F). In order to verify the evolution process of carbon dots at room temperature, time-dependence morphological changes during synthesis were investigated. B-CDs were bulk solids in the initial stage during the synthesis process (Fig. 3 A). From 5 min to 10 min, the huge aggregates became smaller, and after 10 min, the particle size of the nanoparticles was decomposed to about 100 nm (Fig. 3B, C). From 15 min to 20 min, the particle size was further reduced to about 60 nm, the interior of the nanoparticles began to separate and differentiate into quantum dots of about 10 nm (Fig. 3D, E). After 30 min, the chemical process was nearly completed and the particle size was controlled to 5 nm (Fig. 3F). In an analogous mode, G-CDs demonstrated the similar formation route (Fig. 3G–M), with exception that the reaction time could be extended to 120 min. In addition, the UV–vis absorption of carbon dots at different time periods were further monitored. As presented in Fig. S1A, the initial absorption band of B-CDs at 292 nm was mainly attributed to the electronic transitions of m-phenylenediamine itself. As time was increased, the absorption of m-phenylenediamine was changed due to the progression of the amide reaction. From 10 to 15 min, the absorption peak was started to shift to short wavelength. Due to the continuous reaction of carbon dots, the nanoparticle size was substantially reduced, and the UV–vis absorption peak was blue-shifted to 281 nm. The result was in agreement with the transmission electron microscope (abbreviated as TEM) results. After 30 min, the UV–vis absorption band did not change, indicating that the reaction was completed. As demonstrated in Fig. S1B, G-CDs also possessed the absorption peak of m-phenylenediamine at first. Depending on the change of time, the absorption signal was shifted from the 291 nm to 285 nm. After 120 min, the UV absorption band was stable, the end of the reaction was also supported by the TEM results. During the structural evaluation, the main broad band at 25° in XRD curves was corresponding to (0,0,2) crystal plane in both B-CDs and G-CDs [37] (Fig. 4 A). The results indicated the amorphous carbon phase would exist. The appearance of a few sharp peaks in G-CDs suggested that its crystallinity and ordered carbon atoms were better than that of B-CDs. Although the exact nature of the two carbon dots was not well-defined, their structural information could be further clarified via elemental analysis. The employed maleic acid and m-phenylenediamine were selected as two precursors and the synthesis strategy favors a polycondensation process and the amide bond formation would occur (Fig. S2). The main difference between B-CDs and G-CDs would be the length of the polyamide chains. As given in Fig. 4B, the C, N, O element compositions of B-CDs derived from energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) were 69.63, 16.69 and 13.68%, respectively. As for G-CDs, the C element content was 67.28%, the N and O elements were measured to be 17.63 and 15.09%. We also carried out the elemental analysis to explore the chemical nature (Fig. S3). The results revealed that B-CDs would be formed by two reaction molecules in a 1:1 M ratio and the formula of C10H8N2O2 was provided. The nitrogen and oxygen contents in G-CDs were also higher than B-CDs, indicating more CONH units were included in the polymeric network of G-CDs.

The optical behavior of the two carbon dots was investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. In Fig. 5 A, B-CDs gave an absorption band at 281 nm, which was caused by the π-π* transition of the CC conjugate unit. Another absorption signal was located at 372 nm, which was originated from n-π* transitions. According to the absorption profile, we have found the excitation and emission peaks of B-CDs were situated at 372 and 470 nm, respectively. A very bright-blue luminescence was observed under 365 nm UV illumination (Fig. S4A). In Fig. 5B, the excitation-dependent feature was identified. As the excitation wavelength was increased from 360 to 460 nm, the emission peak was shifted from 480 to 537 nm.

Though two organic precursors m-phenylenediamine and maleic acid were exactly the same for B-CDs and G-CDs, their photophysical performance was different. At first, the characteristic absorption peaks of G-CDs were measured to be 285 nm (π-π* transition) and 389 nm (n-π* transition) (Fig. 5C). Its photoluminescence spectra displayed band shifts compared with B-CDs. A striking green band at 502 nm was achieved due to the 365 nm ultra-violet illumination (Fig. S4B). In particular, the emission wavelength of G-CDs was maintained stable excited from 360 nm to 460 nm. Such excitation-independent property has been reported in literature [33] and the high nitrogen/oxygen content or the CONH units caused the red shift of photoluminescence. It has been reported that the surface state especially degree of surface oxidation would be closely related to photophysical properties [38]. The longer wavelength shifts could be relied on the oxygen content within the surface of carbon dots. Since the concentration of oxygen was elevated, the quantities of surface defects increased. Such defects might combine excitons and red shifts for emission bands were afforded. In this contribution, the employment of carboxylic acid and amine would undergo a few steps during carbon dots preparation including condensation to amide units and polymerization. Such polymeric structure might form chains like intermediates and the networks might occur aggregation via covalent, hydrogen bonding and other non-covalent forces and particles were afforded. The nanoparticle included a carbogenic core mainly consisting of sp2 carbon derived from aromatic ring in m-phenylenediamine and amorphous sp3 carbon hosts (aliphatic units in organic precursors). Additionally, the molecular weights of the two samples were analyzed by gel permeation chromatography and the values were determined to be 2150 and 2430 for B-CDs and G-CDs respectively (Fig. S5). The above measurements firmly supported that nitrogen and oxygen contents in G-CDs were larger than B-CDs (Fig. 4B and S3).

Its chemical composition was further explored via XPS studies. The full spectra for the two carbon dots provided in Figs. S6 and S7 exhibited three elemental signals including C1s, N1s and O1s. In high-resolution spectra of B-CDs, three resolved peaks in C1s spectrum were found at 284.4, 285.0 and 286.6 eV, which were attributed to CC/CC, CN/CO and CO groups. The N1s spectrum showed two peaks at 399.1 and 400.0 eV, which were assigned to amino N and pyrrolic N. In O1s spectrum, two bands at 531.4 and 532.5 eV were observed and they were ascribed to CO and CO units. In the sample of G-CDs, C1s band can be deconvoluted into three signals (CC/CC 284.4 eV, CN/CO 285.2 eV and CO 286.1 eV). The N1s can be composed of two peaks at 399.5 eV (amino N) and 400.3 eV (pyrrolic N). Two signals at 531.3 eV (CO) and 532.7 eV (CO) were identified in O1s spectrum. The corresponding improvement in the contents for oxygen and nitrogen units was verified in Table S1, demonstrating the similar results to the energy dispersive spectroscopy and elemental analysis.

Furthermore, the above mentioned excitation-dependent feature would also be derived from emissive surface states. In related literature, it has been reported that λex-dependence of emission curves could be caused by the distribution of diverse emissive sites in the carbon nanostructures [33]. The lifetime evaluation supported B-CDs possessed a bi-exponential decay function (τ1 = 3.81 ns, τ2 = 8.90 ns, average τ = 8.31 ns) and the exponential property of the lifetime would be attributed to different emissive sites (Fig. S8). The decay curve of G-CDs was mono-exponential with lifetimes of 5.81 ns. The combination of carbon core and diverse surface states in B-CDs would lead to the change for the photoluminescence curves upon excitation.

The optical stability of the two carbon dots was further studied. Under strong acid and alkaline conditions, the fluorescence intensity was slightly weaker than that of neutral (Fig. S9A, B). Moreover, the two carbon dots possessed relatively stable signals at pH from 5 to 8, which made it possible for the entry of live cells. As provided in Fig. S9C, D, the emission changes of the two carbon dots were recorded after 48 days. Very negligible changes were monitored and they have significant potentials in the field of bio-analysis.

In recent years, extensive work has been concentrated on the study of tunable photoluminescence of carbon dots [39]. The main factors are summarized as quantum size [40], surface oxidation state [24], surface functional groups [41] and other atomic content [42]. Since there was no substantial difference for the particle size of the two carbon dots, the chemical compositions would be considered for elucidating the emission nature of B-CDs and G-CDs. The surface functional groups of carbon dots were investigated by FT-IR spectra. From the blue line (B-CDs) in Fig. S10, the broad band at 3450 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of the -NH- or OH bonds. The absorption peak at 1686 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the CO bonds. The bands at 1613 cm−1 and 1562 cm−1 were derived from the typical amide I and II components [43]. The absorption band at 1400 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of the CN bonds. These spectral data supported the presence of the –CO-NH- units, so the synthesis of B-CDs was established on the amidation reaction. In the curve of G-CDs, the absorption signals in amide I region were higher than that of B-CDs, such band area improvement would be regarded as the indication of functional group concentration [44].

Moreover, the optical energy band gap values (between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO)) of B-CDs and G-CDs were calculated by UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (Fig. 6 ). Specifically, the optical energy band gap of the B-CDs and G-CDs was decreased from 2.42 eV to 2.23 eV. The results revealed that the energy band gap of the G-CDs was lower than the value of the B-CDs and the emission wavelength was shifted. In view of solvent structure, dimethylformamide with reinforced pull–push effect might be more favorable to attract protons compared with formamid due to the lonely pair electrons on the carbonyl groups. Thus, more m-phenylenediamine could be reacted, resulting in a higher surface oxidation state. Therefore, the tunable luminescence of B-CDs and G-CDs was mainly influenced by two factors. The first would be the amide unit concentration, and the other could be the band gap defect caused by the degree of surface oxidation [31].

Tetracycline (abbreviated as TC) is widely used to treat diseases such as respiratory infections and skin infections [45]. However, the excessive amounts of tetracycline will cause immeasurable results, such as ear, digestive and kidney damage [46]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a strategy capable of reflecting tetracycline in the environment. Due to its low production cost and stable fluorescence performance, it is meaningful to apply such nanoplatforms for the detection of tetracycline. It was found that both B-CDs and G-CDs exhibited optical responses to tetracycline (Fig. S11). In view of correlation coefficient (R2) and detection limit, B-CDs was slightly better than G-CDs. The optical activity of B-CDs in response to tetracycline was explored by fluorometric titrations (Fig. 7 A). When the concentration of analyte was gradually increased (0–23 μM), the emission intensity of B-CDs at 470 nm was significantly suppressed. As given in Fig. S12A, the excitation band of the B-CDs was overlapped with the UV absorption band of tetracycline, which was caused by the internal filter effect (abbreviated as IFE) [47]. We can also observe the overlapping area for B-CDs was much larger than G-CDs, it may explain the reason why B-CDs possessed higher sensitivity.

With the aim of confirming this mechanism, the decay lifetime of B-CDs before and after tetracycline addition was examined. No substantial change was found (Fig. S13A). This fact indicated that no energy transfer occurred during the recognition process and the quenching effect was generated by the IFE. As shown in Fig. 7B, the correlation between (F0-F)/F0 (F0 and F refer to the emission intensity of B-CDs in the absence and presence of tetracycline) and concentration of tetracycline could be fitted by linear algorithm at two concentration ranges including 0–10 μM (Y = 0.066X–0.018, R2 = 0.98) and 11–23 μM (Y = 0.024X + 0.381, R2 = 0.99). Three independent experiments were explored. The standard deviation was determined to be 0.055% and the slope of linear equation was 0.066. The detection limit was calculated as 3 × SD/slope = 3 × 0.055%/0.066 = 25 nM (SD refers to the standard deviation). The sensitivity of the B-CDs was compared with the other reported sensor systems (Table S2). Although B-CDs did not reach the lowest detection limit, the room temperature synthesis strategy saves time and energy.

To further investigate the selectivity of the B-CDs sensing system, we used other analogues (penicillin, ampicillin, streptomycin, lincomycin, cefalexin, hemoglobin, sparfloxacin, marbofloxacin and glucose) under the same conditions for experiments. In Fig. S13B, the results showed that B-CDs had negligible changes to other analogues, indicating that the sensing system possessed excellent specificity. According to the literature, tetracycline is mainly eliminated through the urine after entering the human body [48]. Therefore, it is a key-point to detect tetracycline in human urine. However, urine contains a lot of amino acids (Ala, Asp, Arg, Asn, Cys, Gln, Gly, His, Hcy, Leu, Lys, Ser and Trp, the above full names were given in experimental section) and some common metal ions (Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Na+ and K+), the effect of coexisting substances on the detection of tetracycline was discussed. As provided in Fig. S13C and D, there were almost no apparent changes in fluorescence intensity, indicating that the exogenous coexisting materials had essentially no effects on the optical sensing of tetracycline. In order to prove its real effect, it was used in human urine for standard recovery experiments. As shown in Table S3, tetracycline was measured in the human urine, with recoveries rates ranging from 97.1% to 109.1%. It firmly supported that the B-CDs exhibited promising future in tetracycline bio-medical analysis.

Before real biological evaluation, the Cell Counting Kit-8 (abbreviated as CCK-8) assay was used to assess the cytotoxicity (Fig. S14). From the results, the intake of the two carbon dots had almost no negative effects on cell viability. In this way, the excellent biocompatibility was demonstrated. To further explore the influence on the cells, Annexin V/7-AAD Kit was utilized to study apoptosis by flow cytometry. Comparing with the HeLa cells cultured with B-CDs and the control sample (Fig. 8 A), there was no significant difference in the number of cells in the four different apoptotic periods (Q1 region are early apoptotic periods, Q2 region are late apoptotic periods, Q3 region are damaged periods, Q4 region are live periods). Likewise, the exposure to G-CDs in living cells gave the similar results with B-CDs, indicating that the two carbon dots could be favorable for the application within complex living organisms. Due to its low toxicity and high biological tolerance, we further explored the roles of two carbon dots in fluorescence imaging of Hela cells. After the Hela cells were cultured with the two carbon dots for 6 h, the optical signal was observed by a confocal fluorescence imaging microscope excited by the laser irradiation at 405 nm. In Fig. 9 , both B-CDs and G-CDs passed through the cell membrane and displayed blue or green luminescence in the cells. In view of the spatial distribution of G-CDs in HeLa cells, the green fluorescence was clearly visible in the cytoplasm (Fig. 9). However, the story of B-CDs was slightly different. In the blue light channel, the image showed blue luminescence spread across the whole visual range. Excited by green fluorescent channel, the green emission was observed and the results were consistent with the excitation-dependent photoluminescence. Such carbon dots provided an effective way to determine the sub-cellular structure and information. We have to say that the emission wavelengths for the two carbon dots were still not satisfactory for cellular imaging. But their excitation wavelengths could be extended to visible light range and such long-wavelength irradiation will improve safety for biological tissues. A technical challenge in materials chemistry is to synthesize luminescent species that can be irradiated by visible light. Herein, both the blue and green carbon dots can be excited beyond 400 nm (Fig. S15). As for the optical measurements, we could avoid to use quartz optics when the emissive materials would be excited at wavelengths more than 400 nm. Additionally, the process of visible light sensitization will permit the employment of cheaper semiconductor diode lasers and such light source would be favorable for cellular imaging observations.

Light-emitting diodes (LEDs) are promising devices in recent industry fields and the combination of LED chip and multi-color quantum dots has been performed to generate designated lights [49–56]. The B-CDs powder and G-CDs powder were dispersed in ethanol and dropped on an optical lens. After drying, the optical lens was placed over the LEDs chip (365 nm) to obtain Blue-LEDs (Fig. 10 A Inset) and Green-LEDs (Fig. 10B Inset). Fig. 10A, B included the emission spectra of two LEDs. The emission peak of Blue-LEDs was around 470 nm and the emission peak of Green-LEDs was located at 510 nm, which were consistent with the emission spectra measured by the fluorescence spectrophotometer. Moreover, the CIE chromaticity coordinates of the Blue-LEDs and the Green-LEDs at the current of 80 mA were located at blue and green region (0.1993, 0.2423), (0.3199, 0.5027), respectively (Fig. 10D). The correlated LEDs characteristic parameters were given in Fig. 10C.

In summary, we have developed an eco-friendly method to assemble two carbon dots (B-CDs and G-CDs) and the achieved nanomaterials possessed blue/green emissions, excellent aqueous solubility, enough stability and tolerance to pH from 5 to 8. The advantages for this study could be summarized as follows: i) the bottom-up synthesis was established on a straightforward technique and very common precursors such as maleic acid and m-phenylenediamine were used. The traditional bottom-up strategy generally needed special instruments and most of approaches were energy-consuming [57,58]. ii) the experiments were performed at room temperature for 30 min or 120 min. No excessive inputs of energy/irradiation or high temperature were required and the experimental condition was mild compared with reported publication [59]. iii) As for the reactants, the typical strong oxidants such as sulfuric acid and strong alkali (NaOH) were absent in the experiments. Therefore, such method has been considered to be cost-effective, simple and eco-friendly. iv) it has been found that the replacement of formamid with DMF could control the band shifts from blue to green. The nature of emission color evolvement has been substantiated by surface states. The relevant –CONH– components would be responsible for the shifts of emission wavelengths. v) Tetracycline could cause emission quenching and the detection limit was as low as 25 nM. vi) The two carbon dots were found to be low toxicity and exhibited potentials in cellular-imaging and the fabrication of light-emitting-diodes devices was realized. To our knowledge, it is the first report for the preparation of room-temperature synthesized carbon dots-based blue/green LEDs and the results would provide a new direction for cost-effective assembly of low-dimensional quantum dots with multiple functions.

Gongxi Qiao: Validation, Writing - original draft. Gui Chen: Data curation, Investigation. Qin Wen: Resources, Visualization. Wanqiang Liu: Software. Jinwei Gao: Funding acquisition. Zhiqiang Yu: Project administration. Qianming Wang: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing.
